titleStmt.title: Letter from James Davidson to his mother, Clara Mary Davidson, 21 May 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: James Davidson

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p:
A letter from James Davidson (1877-1916) to his mother, Clara Mary. James details how busy he is with paperwork despite the conditions in the trenches. Due to a change in the weather he asks that she send him light weight clothing.
This letter is part of a large correspondence from Captain James Davidson to his parents. Davidson, who was Managing Director of the Sirocco Engineering Works in Belfast, served with 13 Royal Irish Rifles and the Machine Gun Corps. His letters describe his experiences while training in England and serving in France. He was killed on 1 July 1916, the first day of the Battle of the Somme.

msIdentifier.repository: Public Record Office of Northern Ireland

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
21/5/16 My Dearest Mother — I
have two letters of yours
& two from K. to acknowledge
but am afraid I have
not time to send much
of a reply. The work
seems to increase every
day. Today Sunday
I have been since early
morning in Orderly Room
trying to get things up
to date but letters have
come pouring in all
day calling for further
reports & returns
with the result that I
am very little further
forward. These never
ending returns & c
are heartbreaking
when there is so much
important work waiting
to be done in the Trenches.
One would imagine that
at the front, this office
work would to a
large extent cut out
but it seems quite the
contrary. The weather is now very
warm & I was so glad
to change into my light
underwear. Would
you please send me
out 6 of my light khaki
shirts. I will post
my warm clothing
home some day this
week. The warm
lining for my waterproof
arrived safely, thanks
ever so much dear Mother.
I suppose you received
my British Warm all
right from the officer
who took it for me
when going on leave.
Charlie Murland arrived
out here on Friday &
has joined the 13th again.
He stayed with us till
today & as you can
guess we were all
very delighted to have
him. Have not
seen Gerard lately
but hear he is all
right, though he has
been in a rather nasty
part of the Line recently.
Personally I am
keeping all right — this
warm weather agrees
with me — but I do
long for a few days
complete rest. So far there is no talk
of being relieved though
indeed if we were not
going back some distance
I should prefer to remain
where we are. I feel this is a very
uninteresting letter
Mother dear but there
is really no news —
that is to say — news
that I can tell you.
Will you thank K. for her
letters & with fondest
love to all. Your loving son Jim
On Active Service Inserted 21st 26th May/16 Mrs S C Davidson
Seacourt
Bangor J Davidson
21/6/16
